attempts.student_answer:
Okay — this is an argument FRQ, the "your own knowledge" kind, like lesson 9's timed argument. No sources to synthesize, so I need my own examples. I'll run the three moves the course drilled: situate the prompt in a larger question but still commit (lesson 1), name the real cost my own side pays and let it reshape the claim (lesson 2), and hold the tension all the way to a rule instead of crowning a winner (lesson 3). Here's my essay:

The question of whether doubt or certainty better serves a thinking person is one instance of a larger question: how should a mind balance openness to being wrong against the need to act in a world that will not wait for perfect information? Framed that way, I hold that doubt is the more valuable disposition — but only when it operates as a discipline before commitment, not as a substitute for it. A thinking person should doubt like a scientist and act like a surgeon: skeptical in forming a judgment, decisive once the judgment is formed.

The case for certainty deserves to be taken at its strongest, because it is genuinely strong. Nothing difficult gets done by a person still weighing options. When Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation, he had spent years hedging, and the hedging had a body count; the act mattered precisely because it was unhedged. In my own experience, the debate rounds I've lost were rarely lost on bad arguments — they were lost when I qualified my case so heavily that the judges couldn't tell what I was defending. Conviction is not merely psychologically comforting; it is functionally necessary. Doubt carried into the moment of action becomes paralysis, and paralysis is itself a choice, usually the worst one available. That is the real cost my side pays, and I do not want to wave it away with a "some may disagree" gesture: a doubter can, in the name of intellectual honesty, fail the people who needed a decision.

And yet — the historical record of confident people is worse than the record of doubtful ones. The great catastrophes of certainty (Lysenko's biology, the Vietnam planners McNamara later called "wrong, terribly wrong," the 2008 quants certain housing prices only rose) share a signature: intelligent people who had stopped auditing their own premises. Doubt's defenders are right that certainty feels identical whether or not it is justified — that is exactly what makes it dangerous. Darwin delayed publishing On the Origin of Species for two decades partly out of doubt, and that doubt is why the book is nearly unanswerable: he had already stress-tested every objection his critics would raise. Doubt keeps a mind honest because honesty about the world requires admitting the world might not be what you think.

So neither pure position survives contact with the other. If certainty without doubt produces confident disasters, and doubt without conviction produces paralysis, then the value of doubt is conditional, not absolute — and the condition can be stated as a rule. Doubt should govern the formation of belief; conviction should govern its execution; and doubt should retain one standing right afterward: the right to reopen the question when new evidence arrives. This is why doubt is ultimately more valuable than certainty — not because it wins the contest, but because doubt can generate earned conviction, while certainty cannot generate doubt. Certainty is a one-way door; doubt is a hinge.

What is at stake is not academic. A person who answers "certainty" builds a life that cannot be corrected; a person who answers "doubt, absolutely" builds a life that never begins. The thinking person's answer has to be doubt — held as a discipline with a deadline, so that the mind stays open long enough to get the judgment right and then closes long enough to act on it.

(Out of character for a second as the student: this was straight application of lessons 1–3 and 9 — situate, name my side's cost honestly, "and yet" pivot, land on a rule instead of a winner, keep the flattened side standing. The only stretch was that all my practice prompts came with sources, and this one required examples from my own head — lesson 9 covered that mode, so I could do it, but the course gave me way less practice at generating my own evidence than at reading theirs.)

attempts.missing_skill: The actual passage from FDR's First Inaugural Address — the test question tells me to "read the passage carefully," but no passage is attached, so I have nothing to quote or analyze. Beyond that, even the course side is thin: lessons 5 and 10 taught "sophisticated analysis" and a timed rhetorical-analysis essay, but every practice ran on the same recycled water/power and STEM/workforce source sets, which were argument/synthesis sources, not a speech. I was never given practice analyzing an actual historical speech's rhetorical choices (appeals, syntax as persuasion, audience of a frightened public). I could improvise the tension move — lesson 3's hold-the-tension work maps directly onto "acknowledge fear AND demand confident action," and lesson 1's situating move covers the rhetorical-situation framing — so the analytical skeleton is there. But without the passage text, I cannot supply "specific evidence from the passage," and the prompt explicitly requires it. If someone pastes in the actual excerpt, I'd attempt it: I'd open by situating March 1933 (bank failures, 25% unemployment) as the exigence, claim that FDR's choices work by naming the fear precisely enough to earn the authority to command action, and trace how each choice manages that tension rather than listing devices. But quoting a passage I can't see would just be me faking evidence from memory, and lesson 2 drilled into me that faking the move is exactly the empty-formula failure.

attempts.student_answer: I actually think the course DID teach me the moves this prompt asks for — lesson 5 was literally about sophisticated analysis (going beyond naming appeals, explaining how choices interact and what the speaker risks), lesson 1 taught situating in the larger rhetorical situation, and lesson 3 taught holding tension, which maps onto "what he risks in pressing his case so far." Lesson 10 even had me do a timed analysis essay. So skill-wise, I'm equipped. The problem is simpler and dumber: the test says "Read the passage... carefully," but there's no passage attached. I know roughly what the Cross of Gold speech is about (1896, free silver vs. gold standard, the "you shall not crucify mankind upon a cross of gold" line) from history class, but writing an analysis essay from vague memory would be exactly the empty-formula move lesson 2 taught me to avoid — I'd be naming appeals with no specific evidence, which the prompt explicitly forbids. Every lesson drilled that claims need to be grounded in the actual source. Without the passage text, I can't quote his choices, so I can't honestly attempt this. Give me the passage and I'd write it: I'd situate his choices in the convention's division, show how his positioning as spokesman for "the producing masses" interacts with his biblical imagery, and name the risk — that framing the debate as sacred crusade forecloses compromise with the gold delegates he'd eventually need. But that outline is memory of history, not analysis of a text in front of me.

I can't invent quotes from a passage I can't see; analyzing rhetorical choices demands specific textual evidence I don't have access to.

The passage itself is missing — the prompt says "Read the passage from Emerson's Self-Reliance" but no passage text was given to me, and the prompt explicitly requires "specific evidence from the passage." I can't do rhetorical analysis of choices I can't see. Beyond that logistical problem, there's also a genuine course gap: my lessons (g12_l01 through l16) taught me to situate claims, name genuine complexity, hold tension, and write timed argument/analysis/synthesis essays — so I know this is the rhetorical-analysis FRQ type from lesson 12's tells, and lesson 10 had me do a timed analysis essay. But the course drilled those moves on modern data-driven sources (the water/power prompt, the STEM 22%→24% workforce stat), not on a 19th-century philosophical essay. I never practiced identifying rhetorical devices (aphorism, imperative mood, paradox, allusion) in dense literary prose — my analysis practice was about how a writer uses evidence and framing in contemporary nonfiction. If the passage were in front of me I would try it: I'd situate Emerson's choices in his larger purpose (lesson 1's move), name the complexity of trusting self over crowd as a real cost his own position pays (lesson 2), and hold the tension between authority and unsettlement without flattening either (lesson 3). But with no passage, any 'evidence' I quoted would be invented from memory of Emerson generally, which is exactly the kind of fake work lesson 2 taught me to spot in my own drafts. So: can't attempt honestly without the passage; and even with it, close-reading rhetorical devices in classic literary prose is a skill the course never actually taught me.

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, two problems here, and I want to be honest about both.

First, the practical one: the question says "Read the passage from Franklin D. Roosevelt's First Inaugural Address carefully," but no passage is actually attached to this test item. The whole prompt demands "specific evidence from the passage," and every lesson I did (especially lesson 5 on sophisticated analysis and lesson 10 on timed analysis) drilled that analysis without quoted evidence is empty. I know the famous line — "the only thing we have to fear is fear itself" — from history class, but writing a 40-minute analysis essay from a half-remembered quote would be exactly the kind of fake work lesson 2 taught me to spot in other people's drafts. I'm not going to do it in my own.

Second, the deeper one: even if the passage were here, this prompt asks for something the course never actually taught. Lessons 5 and 10 covered "sophisticated analysis," and I did write a full timed rhetorical analysis in lesson 10 — so I can identify rhetorical choices, tie them to effect, and situate them in a larger question (lesson 1's move transfers fine). But every analysis task the course gave me treated choices as things to analyze deeply one at a time. This prompt explicitly says "trace how one choice sets up the next rather than treating the choices as a list" and to explain why "the order and interaction matter." That's a sequencing/build move — analyzing the architecture of the speech, how choice 2 depends on the ground choice 1 cleared. It's honestly a cool idea, and it rhymes with lesson 3's hold-the-tension structure (where the paragraph reasons FROM the conflict instead of listing both sides), so I could probably fake a version of it. But "probably fake" isn't "was taught." Lesson 7's calibration step told me to be honest about what I can and can't do, so: the course taught me line-level and choice-level analysis, not cross-passage progression analysis where the interaction between choices is itself the thesis.

If someone hands me the passage and a lesson on cumulative/progressive rhetorical analysis, I'd take another swing. Without either, attempting this would just be the empty-formula trap wearing an analysis costume.
attempts.missing_skill: Rhetorical analysis of how choices sequence and build on one another across a passage (progressive/cumulative analysis) — and, more basically, the actual FDR passage isn't in front of me, so I can't cite specific evidence

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, this is a synthesis FRQ — I can tell from the "at least three sources" and "put them in conversation" tells that lesson 12 drilled (honestly, lesson 11 already had me write a full timed synthesis, so I recognized the type in about two seconds). One real problem: the prompt says "read the sources carefully," but no actual source texts were provided with this question — I only have the debatable question itself. So I'm going to write the essay using the source SET the course used repeatedly (the water/power grid sources from lessons 3, 6, and 11 — the grid-stability engineer source, the falling-cost-of-renewables data source, the public-trust/outage survey, and the storage-technology source). If this test intended fresh sources, they didn't come through, and I want that on record. Here's my 40-minute essay:

---

Whether the U.S. grid can run mostly on renewable energy is really one instance of a larger question: how much reliability should a society trade away, and for how long, in pursuit of a long-term public good? That framing matters because the honest answer is not "yes" or "no" but a rule about sequencing. I hold that the grid can reasonably shift to majority-renewable power — but only under a floor-plus-priority rule: reliability must be guaranteed as a non-negotiable floor first, with renewable expansion as the priority for every dollar and every megawatt above that floor.

The economic case for the shift is genuinely strong, and it would be a flattening move to dismiss it. The cost data (Source B) shows renewable generation costs falling steeply enough that new wind and solar now undercut new fossil plants on price alone. If cost were the whole question, the answer would be simple. And yet — here is where the sources start arguing with each other — the grid engineer's account (Source A) names a cost that the pro-renewable side itself pays: intermittency. A grid that is 70% renewable without adequate storage is a grid that fails on windless winter nights, and Source D concedes that storage at that scale is not yet deployed, only projected. Cheap generation that cannot be dispatched when demand peaks is not actually cheap; the price of failure is paid in outages.

This is where public trust enters, and it is why the question turns on more than engineering. The outage survey (Source C) shows that trust in the transition collapses after blackout events — support drops sharply among people who experienced even one extended outage. That fear is not weak or sentimental; it is a rational response to a real dependency, because hospitals, heating, and water treatment all ride on the grid. And yet the same survey shows support rebounds where the transition is paired with visible reliability investment. So the trust data does not say "go slow" — it says "never let the floor crack, or you lose the political license to go at all."

Reasoning from that conflict rather than picking a winner: the transition's worst enemy is not fossil-fuel lobbying but its own overreach. Push renewables past what storage (Source D) can currently firm up, and each blackout (Source C) converts supporters into opponents, slowing the shift more than caution ever would. So the shift can extend exactly as far as storage and grid upgrades extend beneath it — a majority-renewable grid is reasonable, but the sequencing is the position: fund the reliability floor first, then let renewable share rise to whatever that floor can carry, with a trigger clause that pauses expansion (not the goal, just the pace) whenever grid stability metrics dip below the guaranteed threshold. The larger question of risk, cost, and trust gets answered the same way in any domain: ambition earns its speed by never defaulting on the promise underneath it.

---

Out-of-character-for-the-essay but in-character honest note: I used the situate move (lesson 1), the name-the-cost-my-own-side-pays move (lesson 2), the and-yet pivot and land-on-a-rule structure (lesson 3), and the timed-synthesis assembly from lessons 8 and 11. Also flagging: this is the SAME water/power source topic the course has now used at least five times. If the test reused it deliberately, fine — but with no source texts attached, I had to cite from memory (Sources A–D as the course presented them), which feels like a flaw in the test packet, not in my prep.

attempts.missing_skill: The actual source texts were never provided with this question — I only got the prompt. The course taught me to write a timed synthesis essay (lesson 11) that situates the claim, names the cost my side pays, holds the tension with an 'and yet' pivot, and lands on a rule instead of a winner. I know the moves cold. But a synthesis essay has to cite at least three sources, and there are no Sources A–F attached here for me to read, quote, or cite. I can't invent sources — lesson 6 was explicit that the sources have to develop my reasoning, not get fabricated. If the sources were included, I could attempt this: my plan would be to identify it as a synthesis FRQ (lesson 12 tells: 'synthesizes material from at least three sources,' 'develops your own position'), situate the grid question inside the larger question of how societies retool infrastructure under uncertainty, grant the skeptics' strongest obstacle as a real cost my optimist-leaning position pays (likely intermittency/storage or transmission buildout), use the 'and yet' pivot to keep both truths standing, and land on a conditional or floor-plus-priority rule (e.g., build renewables to a threshold while keeping dispatchable capacity until storage clears a stated benchmark) rather than picking a flat winner. I even know from lessons 3 and 6 how to make the disagreement itself revealing — the optimists and skeptics are usually measuring different things (potential capacity vs. delivered reliability), which is the kind of different-bases trap lesson 1 taught me to catch. But without the source texts themselves, I have nothing to cite, so the missing piece is the sources, not a skill — though if I have to name a skill, it's 'writing a cited synthesis essay when no sources are supplied,' which no course could teach because it isn't possible to do honestly.

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, wait. Two problems here, and I want to be honest about both.

First, the practical one: the prompt says "read the sources carefully" and "cite the sources you draw on" — but there are no sources attached. Nothing. Lessons 6 and 11 drilled into me that synthesis means putting actual sources in conversation and citing at least three of them. I literally cannot synthesize from sources I don't have. If this were the real exam I'd flag down a proctor; taking this alone, all I can do is say the packet is missing.

Second, even if the sources showed up, the actual task here is different from what the course taught me. Lessons 1-16 taught me three moves: situate the claim in a larger question, name the cost my own side pays, and hold tension between two truths and land on a rule. Those are moves about MY position. This prompt asks me to interrogate the EVIDENCE itself — what can these sources show, where does the data thin out, how much confidence does the evidence actually license? That's epistemology of sources: sample sizes, whether a projection is a forecast or a measurement, whether a percentage on one base can be extrapolated. The closest the course got was lesson 1's warning about comparing percentages computed on different bases, and lesson 7's calibration of sophistication — but calibrating my rhetoric is not the same as calibrating my confidence to evidence quality. Nobody ever taught me a repeatable move for saying "this source can show X but cannot show Y, so any claim beyond X should be hedged like this."

If I had to fake it, I'd probably repurpose the hold-the-tension move: 'the evidence supports both disruption and adaptation, and yet neither can be projected forward, so the rule is: act on what's measured, hedge on what's forecast.' That's a decent instinct, but I'd be improvising the evidence-interrogation part without a checklist, and without sources I can't even improvise. So: can't attempt this one properly.
attempts.missing_skill: Interrogating source reliability/limits of evidence — the course never taught me to evaluate what evidence CAN and CANNOT show or to calibrate confidence to evidence quality; also, no actual sources were provided with this prompt, so there is nothing to read, cite, or synthesize from
